stem: The ellipse \( \dfrac{x^{2}}{[[a]]^{2}}+\dfrac{y^{2}}{[[b]]^{2}}=1 \) has \( [[a]]>[[b]] \). Find its eccentricity \( e \).
solution: For this ellipse, \( c=\sqrt{a^{2}-b^{2}} \) and the eccentricity is \( e=\dfrac{c}{a}=\dfrac{\sqrt{[[a]]^{2}-[[b]]^{2}}}{[[a]]}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( b\) instead of \( a\) in the eccentricity formula.
This uses the hyperbola relation \( c=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}\) instead of the ellipse relation.
This reports \( b/a\) directly instead of \( c/a\).
This forgets to take the square root, reporting \( e^{2}\) instead of \( e\).
This is off by a small additive amount from the correct eccentricity.
This is off by a small additive amount (other direction) from the correct eccentricity.

stem: The ellipse \( \dfrac{x^{2}}{[[a]]^{2}}+\dfrac{y^{2}}{[[b]]^{2}}=1 \) has foci at \( (\pm c,0) \). Find \( c \).
solution: For an ellipse with \( a>b \), the focal distance is \( c=\sqrt{a^{2}-b^{2}}=\sqrt{[[a]]^{2}-[[b]]^{2}}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the hyperbola relation \( c=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}\) instead of the ellipse relation.
This swaps the order of subtraction under the square root, which would be negative here.
This subtracts the semi-axes directly instead of using the Pythagorean-style relation.
This forgets to take the square root, reporting \( c^{2}\) instead of \( c\).
This is off by one from the correct focal distance.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct focal distance.

stem: The hyperbola \( \dfrac{x^{2}}{[[a]]^{2}}-\dfrac{y^{2}}{[[b]]^{2}}=1 \) has vertices at \( (\pm [[a]],0) \). Find its eccentricity \( e \).
solution: For this hyperbola, \( c=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}} \) and the eccentricity is \( e=\dfrac{c}{a}=\dfrac{\sqrt{[[a]]^{2}+[[b]]^{2}}}{[[a]]}=[[answer]] \) (always \( >1 \) for a hyperbola).
distractor_rationales: This uses the ellipse relation \( c=\sqrt{a^{2}-b^{2}}\) instead of the hyperbola relation.
This divides by \( b\) instead of \( a\) in the eccentricity formula.
This reports \( b/a\) directly instead of \( c/a\).
This forgets to take the square root, reporting \( e^{2}\) instead of \( e\).
This is off by a small additive amount from the correct eccentricity.
This is off by a small additive amount (other direction) from the correct eccentricity.

stem: The hyperbola \( \dfrac{x^{2}}{[[a]]^{2}}-\dfrac{y^{2}}{[[b]]^{2}}=1 \) has foci at \( (\pm c,0) \). Find \( c \).
solution: For a hyperbola, the focal distance is \( c=\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}}=\sqrt{[[a]]^{2}+[[b]]^{2}}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the ellipse relation \( c=\sqrt{a^{2}-b^{2}}\) instead of the hyperbola relation.
This takes the absolute difference under the root instead of the sum.
This adds the semi-axes directly instead of using the Pythagorean-style relation.
This forgets to take the square root, reporting \( c^{2}\) instead of \( c\).
This is off by one from the correct focal distance.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct focal distance.

stem: For the ellipse \( \dfrac{x^{2}}{[[a]]^{2}}+\dfrac{y^{2}}{[[b]]^{2}}=1 \) with foci \( F_1,F_2 \), a point \( P \) lies on the ellipse. By the defining property of an ellipse, what is \( PF_1+PF_2 \)?
solution: By definition, the sum of the distances from any point on an ellipse to its two foci is constant and equal to \( 2a \), the length of the major axis: \( PF_1+PF_2=2([[a]])=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the semi-major axis \( a\) instead of the full major axis \( 2a\).
This uses the semi-minor axis \( b\) instead of the semi-major axis \( a\).
This adds the two semi-axes instead of doubling the semi-major axis.
This uses a factor of \( 4\) instead of \( 2\).
This is off by one from the correct constant sum.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct constant sum.

stem: The ellipse \( \dfrac{(x-[[h]])^{2}}{[[a]]^{2}}+\dfrac{(y-[[k]])^{2}}{[[b]]^{2}}=1 \) is centered at \( ([[h]],[[k]]) \), with major axis along the \( x \)-direction. Find the \( x \)-coordinate of its right vertex.
solution: The right vertex sits \( a \) units to the right of the center along the major axis, so its \( x \)-coordinate is \( h+a=[[h]]+[[a]]=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This moves left instead of right from the center.
This uses the semi-minor axis \( b\) instead of the semi-major axis \( a\).
This omits the center's shift \( h\) entirely.
This multiplies \( h\) and \( a\) instead of adding them.
This is off by one from the correct coordinate.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct coordinate.
